statement: The market price of permanent cod quota in Iceland has risen substantially over time, reaching approximately ISK 6,000–7,000 per cod-equivalent kilogram in 2024/2025. The total estimated market value of all permanent fishing quota in Iceland is on the order of ISK 800–1,000 billion (EUR 5–6 billion), comparable to the country's annual GDP.
caveats: Quota market values are difficult to verify precisely as not all transactions are publicly reported. The high capital value of quota is a key argument for those who argue the resource rent is insufficiently taxed. EU membership could significantly affect quota valuations depending on access arrangements.

related_entries.statement: As of the 2024/2025 fishing year, the three largest quota holders in Iceland controlled approximately 45–50% of the total cod-equivalent quota. The ten largest holders controlled roughly 70%. Quota ownership has consolidated steadily since the ITQ system became permanent in 1990, when the top ten held approximately 35% of total quota.

Iceland operates an Individual Transferable Quota (ITQ) system established in 1984 and made permanent in 1990. Under this system, fishing quotas are allocated as permanent shares of the total allowable catch (TAC) that can be bought, sold, and leased. The system has been credited with reducing overcapacity but criticised for concentrating quota ownership.
